text: A 2-week gait retraining program with real-time visual feedback reduces running-related injury occurrence by 62% over 12 months compared to controls.
Gait retraining targeting vertical loading rates produces large reductions in impact loading with a Cohen's d greater than 0.99.
Without gait intervention, up to 38% of novice runners sustain a running-related musculoskeletal injury within 12 months.
Gait-retrained novice runners had only a 16% injury occurrence over 12 months compared to 38% in controls.
Up to 85% of novice distance runners incur a running-related injury in a given year.
Real-time visual feedback on running gait is an effective modality for teaching runners to reduce vertical impact loading rates.
Gait retraining benefits persist over at least 12 months, suggesting durable motor learning from a short 2-week intervention.
